annotations.label: Analysis of algorithms

anchor.snippet: the analysis of algorithms is the process of finding the computational complexity of algorithms

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of computational complexity analysis as a process; it only provides asymptotic comparison of functions.

annotations.label: Efficient algorithm

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to be efficient when this function's values are small

annotations.note: No definition of algorithmic efficiency exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary search runs in logarithmic time

anchor.snippet: binary search is said to run in a number of steps proportional to the logarithm

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize binary search or any logarithmic-time complexity bound.

annotations.label: Hidden constant

anchor.snippet: related by a constant multiplicative factor called a hidden constant

annotations.note: IsBigOWith c makes the multiplicative constant c explicit (‖f‖ ≤ c‖g‖), but 'hidden constant' is not itself a named notion.

annotations.label: Model of computation

anchor.snippet: concerning the particular implementation of the algorithm, called a model of computation

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Turing machines as a model of computation but has no general notion of 'model of computation' nor cost models.

annotations.label: Binary search time bound on n elements

anchor.snippet: if the sorted list to which we apply binary search has n elements

annotations.note: No binary search timing bound is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform cost model

anchor.snippet: the uniform cost model , also called unit-cost model

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of cost models of computation.

annotations.label: Logarithmic cost model

anchor.snippet: the logarithmic cost model , also called logarithmic-cost measurement

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of cost models of computation.

annotations.label: Run-time analysis

anchor.snippet: Run-time analysis is a theoretical classification that estimates and anticipates

annotations.note: No notion of run-time estimation is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear vs binary search on two computers

anchor.snippet: Take as an example a program that looks up a specific entry in a sorted list

annotations.note: This empirical comparison example has no counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order of growth (Big O)

anchor.snippet: an algorithm can be said to exhibit a growth rate on the order of a mathematical function

annotations.note: IsBigO l f g is exactly the big-O 'order of growth' relation between functions along a filter.

annotations.label: Insertion sort is O(n^2)

anchor.snippet: since the run-time of insertion sort grows quadratically as its input size increases

annotations.note: Mathlib defines List.insertionSort and proves its correctness but does not analyze its O(n²) run-time.

annotations.label: Quicksort average-case O(n log n)
annotations.note: Mathlib has no quicksort definition nor any average-case complexity analysis.

annotations.label: Power rule for empirical order of growth

anchor.snippet: Assuming the run-time follows the power rule

annotations.note: Empirical determination of growth order via the power rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algorithm run-time is O(n^2)

anchor.snippet: so one can conclude that f ( n ) = O ( n 2 )

annotations.note: The claim f(n) = O(n²) is expressible via IsBigO atTop f (fun n => n^2), but this particular algorithm's bound is not proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential memory consumption O(2^n)

anchor.section: Growth rate analysis of other resources
anchor.snippet: as the file size n increases, memory will be consumed at an exponential growth rate

annotations.note: Exponential growth O(2^n) is expressible via IsBigO, but this specific memory-consumption example is not formalized.

annotations.label: All practical algorithms are O(1) for large enough constant

anchor.snippet: all practical algorithms are O (1) for a large enough constant

annotations.note: This is an informal observation about bounded inputs with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Hybrid algorithm Timsort

anchor.snippet: This is particularly used in hybrid algorithms

annotations.note: Timsort and hybrid sorting algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.
